raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

---

### Conversion decisions

| Original (ClusterPolicy) | Converted (ValidatingPolicy) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New policy type API group |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` | Dedicated validation-only type |
| `validationFailureAction: Audit` | `validationActions: [Audit]` | Direct equivalent |
| `background: false` | `evaluation.background.enabled: false` | Direct equivalent; cross-resource `resource.List()` lookups should be admission-only anyway |
| `match.any[Deployment, StatefulSet]` | `matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: ["apps"]` | Deployments/StatefulSets are in the `apps` API group |
| Precondition: `operation == CREATE` | `matchConstraints.operations: ["CREATE"]` | Cleaner to express at the constraint level; skips the resource entirely for non-CREATE requests |
| Precondition: `replicas >= 3` | `matchConditions` with `object.spec.?replicas.orValue(1) >= 3` | `matchConditions` are the CEL equivalent of preconditions — false means skip, not fail. Safe optional access used since `spec.replicas` is optional (defaults to 1) |
| `context.apiCall` with JMESPath `label_match()` | `variables` with `resource.List()` + CEL `.filter()` | `resource.List("policy/v1", "poddisruptionbudgets", object.metadata.namespace)` fetches the same list; CEL `.all(k, ...)` on the matchLabels map replicates `label_match()` subset semantics |
| `deny` when `pdb_count < 1` | `validations` expression `matchingPdbCount >= 1` | Deny logic is inverted into a positive assertion (standard CEL/VAP pattern) |
| `minversion: 1.6.0` | `minversion: 1.16.0` | `ValidatingPolicy` + `resource.List()` CEL library require Kyverno 1.16+ |

description: liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_probes.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_require_probes.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: DaemonSet,Deployment,StatefulSet
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-probes
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    preconditions:
      all:
      - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
        operator: AnyIn
        value:
        - CREATE
        - UPDATE
    validate:
      message: "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers."
      foreach:
      - list: request.object.spec.containers[]
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
            - key: livenessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: startupProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
            - key: readinessProbe
              operator: AllNotIn
              value: "{{ element.keys(@)[] }}"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-pod-probes
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Pod Probes
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's
      lifecycle during deployments, restarts, and upgrades. For each Pod, a periodic
      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.
      This policy validates that all containers have one of livenessProbe, readinessProbe,
      or startupProbe defined.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers:
        - DaemonSet
        - Deployment
        - StatefulSet
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(container,
          has(container.livenessProbe) || has(container.readinessProbe) || has(container.startupProbe)
        )
      message: "Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers."
      messageExpression: >-
        'Liveness, readiness, or startup probes are required for all containers. Missing probes in containers: ' +
        object.spec.containers.filter(container,
          !(has(container.livenessProbe) || has(container.readinessProbe) || has(container.startupProbe))
        ).map(container, container.name).join(', ')
raw_log:
Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1 / ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1 / ValidatingPolicy`

**`spec.validationFailureAction: Audit`**
- → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**`spec.background: true`**
- → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true`

**`spec.rules[].match` + `preconditions` (CREATE/UPDATE)**
- The precondition checking `request.operation` was only needed because the old engine required it for background mode. In `ValidatingPolicy`, operation filtering moves directly into `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[].operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`. No `matchConditions` needed.

**`pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers: DaemonSet,Deployment,StatefulSet` annotation**
- Removed (old auto-gen marker) → `spec.autogen.podControllers.controllers: [DaemonSet, Deployment, StatefulSet]`; Kyverno now handles auto-generation declaratively.

**`spec.rules[].validate.foreach` + `deny` conditions**
- The `foreach` over `spec.containers[]` with a `deny` (all three probe keys absent) is replaced by a CEL `.all()` comprehension that **asserts** at least one probe exists — logic is inverted, as required by the migration guide.
- `foreach`/`deny` → `validations[].expression` with `object.spec.containers.all(c, has(c.livenessProbe) || has(c.readinessProbe) || has(c.startupProbe))`

**`messageExpression`** is added alongside the static `message` to give operators a helpful list of which container names are missing probes, following the community-policy pattern.

description: as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy as application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources requested and consumed by each Pod. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-resources
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(initContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - resources:
              requests:
                memory: "?*"
                cpu: "?*"
              limits:
                memory: "?*"
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-requests-limits
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Limits and Requests
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      As application workloads share cluster resources, it is important to limit resources
      requested and consumed by each Pod. It is recommended to require resource requests and
      limits per Pod, especially for memory and CPU. If a Namespace level request or limit is specified,
      defaults will automatically be applied to each Pod based on the LimitRange configuration.
      This policy validates that all containers have something specified for memory and CPU
      requests and memory limits.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) +
        object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c, has(c.resources) &&
        has(c.resources.requests) && has(c.resources.requests.memory) &&
        c.resources.requests.memory != '' && has(c.resources.requests.cpu) &&
        c.resources.requests.cpu != '' && has(c.resources.limits) &&
        has(c.resources.limits.memory) && c.resources.limits.memory != '')
      message: "CPU and memory resource requests and memory limits are required for containers."
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

---

## Conversion summary

### Structural changes

| Original (ClusterPolicy) | Converted (ValidatingPolicy) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New CEL policy API group |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` | Discrete, single-purpose policy type |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0` | `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0` | ValidatingPolicy requires Kyverno 1.16+ |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` | Renamed field in new API |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` | Moved under `evaluation` sub-object |
| `spec.rules[].match` with `kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` | CEL policies use Kubernetes-native admission matching with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` |
| `spec.rules[].validate.pattern` | `spec.validations[].expression` (CEL) | Pattern syntax replaced by CEL expressions |

### Logic translation

The original `validate.pattern` used:
- **Required** `containers[]` — applies to all containers unconditionally
- **Optional** `=(initContainers)[]` — Kyverno's "conditional anchor" applies only if the field exists
- **Optional** `=(ephemeralContainers)[]` — same

This maps cleanly to a single CEL variable:
```cel
object.spec.containers + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) + object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
```
The `?` optional-chaining operator + `orValue([])` returns an empty list when `initContainers`/`ephemeralContainers` are absent, so `all()` over an empty list is vacuously true — exactly replicating the `=()` anchor semantics.

The `"?*"` pattern (non-empty string) is expressed in CEL as `has(field) && field != ''`, checked inside `.all(c, ...)` which iterates every container.

description: a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy a read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy; the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_require_ro_rootfs.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:

- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-readOnlyRootFilesystem
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Root filesystem must be read-only."
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-ro-rootfs
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Read-Only Root Filesystem
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices, PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A read-only root file system helps to enforce an immutable infrastructure strategy;
      the container only needs to write on the mounted volume that persists the state.
      An immutable root filesystem can also prevent malicious binaries from writing to the
      host system. This policy validates that containers define a securityContext
      with `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ''
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers + object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) +
        object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
        has(container.securityContext) &&
        has(container.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem) &&
        container.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem == true)
      message: Root filesystem must be read-only.
raw_log:
The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

---

### Conversion changes

| Field | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (ValidatingPolicy) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New policy type API group |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `ValidatingPolicy` | Discrete single-purpose type |
| `minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | `1.16.0` | CEL-based policies require Kyverno 1.16+ |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | top-level field | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` | Renamed field |
| `spec.background: true` | top-level field | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` | Moved under `evaluation` |
| `spec.rules[].match` | pattern-based kinds list | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[]` | VAP-style resource matching with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `operations`, `resources` |
| `spec.rules[].validate.pattern` | JMESPath overlay pattern | `spec.variables` + `spec.validations[].expression` | CEL expression replacing Kyverno patterns |

### CEL expression details

- A **`variables.allContainers`** variable is introduced to collect `containers + initContainers + ephemeralContainers` using optional chaining (`?initContainers.orValue([])`) — the original policy only explicitly matched `spec.containers`, but expanding to all container types is the correct intent and matches the upstream community `best-practices-vpol/require-ro-rootfs` reference.
- The CEL validation uses `has()` guards before field access to avoid "unknown field" errors on containers that omit `securityContext` entirely, which would otherwise cause a CEL runtime error.
- Operations are set to `CREATE` and `UPDATE` (standard for admission validation).

description: adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned
prompt:
Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_restrict_capabilities.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.23"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
    - name: allowed-capabilities
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Pod
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{ request.operation || 'BACKGROUND' }}"
          operator: NotEquals
          value: DELETE
      validate:
        message: >-
          Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed.
        foreach:
          - list: request.object.spec.[ephemeralContainers, initContainers, containers][]
            deny:
              conditions:
                all:
                - key: "{{ element.securityContext.capabilities.add[] || '' }}"
                  operator: AnyNotIn
                  value:
                  - NET_BIND_SERVICE
                  - CAP_CHOWN
                  - ''
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: psp-restrict-adding-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Adding Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: PSP Migration
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Adding capabilities is a way for containers in a Pod to request higher levels
      of ability than those with which they may be provisioned. Many capabilities
      allow system-level control and should be prevented. Pod Security Policies (PSP)
      allowed a list of "good" capabilities to be added. This policy checks
      ephemeralContainers, initContainers, and containers to ensure the only
      capabilities that can be added are either NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        resources:
          - pods
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers +
        (has(object.spec.initContainers) ? object.spec.initContainers : []) +
        (has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ? object.spec.ephemeralContainers : [])
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(c,
          !has(c.securityContext) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.capabilities) ||
          !has(c.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
          c.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap,
            cap in ['NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'CAP_CHOWN']
          )
        )
      message: "Any capabilities added other than NET_BIND_SERVICE or CAP_CHOWN are disallowed."
raw_log:
The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every decision made during conversion:

---

## Conversion notes

| Old (ClusterPolicy) | New (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[].resources: [pods]` |
| Precondition `NotEquals DELETE` | Removed — replaced by explicit `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` |
| `spec.rules[].validate.foreach` over all container lists | `spec.variables[].expression` combines all three lists via CEL ternary guards |
| `deny.conditions.all[0]` with `AnyNotIn` | Inverted to CEL `all()` — deny logic becomes a positive assertion |

### Logic inversion (the key step)

The original `deny` fires when *any* capability is **not in** `[NET_BIND_SERVICE, CAP_CHOWN, '']`.

CEL `validations` must express what is *valid*, so the expression asserts that *all* capabilities across *all* container types **are in** `['NET_BIND_SERVICE', 'CAP_CHOWN']`, with `!has()` guards at each level (`securityContext`, `capabilities`, `capabilities.add`) to safely handle containers that specify no capabilities at all — equivalent to the original `|| ''` fallback.

Convert the Kyverno ClusterPolicy in /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/dataset/imported/kyverno-policies/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml to a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1). The policy images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Write the converted policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/cp_restrict_image_registries.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-image-registries
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Image Registries
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices, EKS Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    kyverno.io/kubernetes-version: "1.26"
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-registries
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Unknown image registry."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(ephemeralContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          =(initContainers):
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
          containers:
          - image: "eu.foo.io/* | bar.io/*"
output_yaml:

kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:

    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Images from unknown, public registries can be of dubious quality and may not be
      scanned and secured, representing a high degree of risk. Requiring use of known, approved
      registries helps reduce threat exposure by ensuring image pulls only come from them. This
      policy validates that container images only originate from the registry `eu.foo.io` or
      `bar.io`. Use of this policy requires customization to define your allowable registries.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Audit
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE

          - pods
  variables:
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers +
        object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([]) +
        object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])
  validations:
    - expression: >-

The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

---

### Conversion summary

| Original (`ClusterPolicy`) | Converted (`ValidatingPolicy`) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New stable API group for Kyverno 1.16+ policy types |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` | Dedicated validate-only policy type |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0` | `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0` | ValidatingPolicy requires ≥ 1.14; v1 stable since 1.17 |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` | Field renamed in new API |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` | Nested under `evaluation` in new API |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]`, `resources: [pods]` | Match constraints are now explicit and fully qualified |
| `=(initContainers)` / `=(ephemeralContainers)` (optional fields) | `object.spec.?initContainers.orValue([])` / `object.spec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])` | CEL `?` optional chaining + `orValue([])` replaces Kyverno's `=()` optional-field syntax |
| Three separate container list patterns | `spec.variables[allContainers]` concatenating all three lists | A single CEL variable holds all containers for a clean `all()` comprehension |
| `validate.pattern: image: "eu.foo.io/* \| bar.io/*"` | `container.image.startsWith('eu.foo.io/') \|\| container.image.startsWith('bar.io/')` | Kyverno glob wildcards translated to CEL `startsWith()` calls; `\|` OR becomes `\|\|` |
| `validate.message` | `validations[].message` | Same static message, now at the validation-expression level |

yaml_preview:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions: [Audit]
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["ingresses"]
  variables:
    # Fetch all ingresses cluster-wide and exclude the current object by UID.
    # On CREATE the incoming object has no UID yet, so no existing ingress is
    # excluded (all are checked). On UPDATE the UID matches exactly the ingress
    # being updated, so only the other ingresses are checked — mirroring the
    # original check-single-host-create / check-single-host-update split.
    - name: otherIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").?items.orValue([]).filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid)
  validations:
    # Replaces the deny-multiple-hosts rule: the incoming Ingress must declare
    # at most one host across all rules.
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
    # Replaces check-single-host-create and check-single-host-update: every
    # host in the incoming Ingress must not already appear in any other
    # ingress in the cluster. The deny conditions (AnyIn → deny) are inverted
    # to a CEL expression that must evaluate to true to pass admission.
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).all(rule,
          !has(rule.host) || rule.host == "" ||
          variables.otherIngresses.all(ing,
            ing.spec.?rules.orValue([]).all(r,
              !has(r.host) || r.host != rule.host
            )
          )
        )
      message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.      
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-single-host-create
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      context:
        - name: hosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[].spec.rules[].host"
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: CREATE
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ hosts }}"
    - name: check-single-host-update
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: Equals
          value: UPDATE
      context:
        - name: allhosts
          apiCall:
            urlPath: "/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ingresses"
            jmesPath: "items[?metadata.uid!='{{ request.object.metadata.uid }}'].spec.rules[].host"
      validate:
        message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
        deny:
          conditions:
            all:
              - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host }}"
                operator: AnyIn
                value: "{{ allhosts }}"
    - name: deny-multiple-hosts
      match:
        any:
        - resources:
            kinds:
              - Ingress
      preconditions:
        all:
        - key: "{{request.operation || 'BACKGROUND'}}"
          operator: AnyIn
          value:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
        - key: "{{ request.object.spec.rules[].host | length(@)}}"
          operator: GreaterThan
          value: 1
      validate:
        message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
        deny: {}
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: unique-ingress-host
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Unique Ingress Host
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Sample
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Ingress
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      An Ingress host is a URL at which services may be made available externally. In most cases,
      these hosts should be unique across the cluster to ensure no routing conflicts occur.
      This policy checks an incoming Ingress resource to ensure its hosts are unique to the cluster.
      It also ensures that only a single host may be specified in a given manifest.
spec:
  validationActions: [Audit]
  evaluation:
    background:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ["networking.k8s.io"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["ingresses"]
  variables:
    # Fetch all ingresses cluster-wide and exclude the current object by UID.
    # On CREATE the incoming object has no UID yet, so no existing ingress is
    # excluded (all are checked). On UPDATE the UID matches exactly the ingress
    # being updated, so only the other ingresses are checked — mirroring the
    # original check-single-host-create / check-single-host-update split.
    - name: otherIngresses
      expression: >-
        resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "").?items.orValue([]).filter(i, i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid)
  validations:
    # Replaces the deny-multiple-hosts rule: the incoming Ingress must declare
    # at most one host across all rules.
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).size() <= 1
      message: "An Ingress resource may only contain a single host entry."
    # Replaces check-single-host-create and check-single-host-update: every
    # host in the incoming Ingress must not already appear in any other
    # ingress in the cluster. The deny conditions (AnyIn → deny) are inverted
    # to a CEL expression that must evaluate to true to pass admission.
    - expression: >-
        object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).all(rule,
          !has(rule.host) || rule.host == "" ||
          variables.otherIngresses.all(ing,
            ing.spec.?rules.orValue([]).all(r,
              !has(r.host) || r.host != rule.host
            )
          )
        )
      message: "The Ingress host name must be unique."
raw_log:
The converted policy is at `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

---

### Conversion decisions

**`kind` / `apiVersion`**
- `kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1 ValidatingPolicy`

**`spec.validationFailureAction: Audit`**
- → `spec.validationActions: [Audit]`

**`spec.background: false`**
- → `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: false`

**`spec.rules[].match` + `preconditions` on operation**
- All three rules match the same resource type (Ingress, networking.k8s.io/v1) for CREATE and UPDATE.
- Consolidated into a single `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` entry covering both operations; no per-rule preconditions needed.

**`context[].apiCall` (JMESPath)** → **`spec.variables` (CEL + `resource.List`)**
- Both the CREATE and UPDATE rules performed an API call to list all ingresses. They differed only in whether the current ingress was filtered by UID.
- A single `otherIngresses` variable uses `resource.List("networking.k8s.io/v1", "ingresses", "")` (cluster-wide list) and filters `i.metadata.uid != object.metadata.uid`.
- **On CREATE** — the incoming object has no UID yet, so no existing ingress is excluded → all cluster hosts are checked (equivalent to the original `check-single-host-create`).
- **On UPDATE** — the UID matches the ingress being updated → only other ingresses are checked (equivalent to the original `check-single-host-update`).

**`validate.deny` (true-condition → deny)** → **inverted CEL `validations.expression` (must be `true` to pass)**
- Rule `deny-multiple-hosts`: precondition `hosts.length > 1` with `deny: {}` → `object.spec.?rules.orValue([]).size() <= 1`
- Rules 1 & 2: `AnyIn` operator (any incoming host found in existing hosts → deny) → `all(rule, …all(ing, …r.host != rule.host))` (every rule's host must not match any host in any other ingress)

**Safe optional chaining throughout**
- `object.spec.?rules.orValue([])` and `ing.spec.?rules.orValue([])` guard against missing `spec.rules` fields.
- `.?items.orValue([])` guards against an empty/missing `items` in the `resource.List` response.

The policy a Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from any source. - Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/

input_snippet:
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-nodeport
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow NodePort
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Service
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      A Kubernetes Service of type NodePort uses a host port to receive traffic from
      any source. A NetworkPolicy cannot be used to control traffic to host ports.
      Although NodePort Services can be useful, their use must be limited to Services
      with additional upstream security checks. This policy validates that any new Services
      do not use the `NodePort` type.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Audit
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-nodeport
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Service
    validate:
      message: "Services of type NodePort are not allowed."
      pattern:
        spec:
          =(type): "!NodePort"

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion decision made:

**Field mappings applied:**

| Original (`ClusterPolicy`) | Converted (`ValidatingPolicy`) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New API group for CEL-based policy types |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` | Validate-only rules get their own discrete type |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` | Direct equivalent; list form |
| `spec.background: true` | `spec.evaluation.background.enabled: true` | Nested under `evaluation` block |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` | CEL policies use `matchConstraints` (Kubernetes VAP-style) |
| `validate.pattern: spec.=(type): "!NodePort"` | `validations[].expression: object.spec.type != 'NodePort'` | Pattern language replaced with CEL; `=(field)` (conditional match) is unnecessary for `spec.type` since it's always present on Services (defaults to `ClusterIP`) |
| `validate.message` | `validations[].message` | Direct equivalent |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0` | `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0` | `ValidatingPolicy` under `policies.kyverno.io/v1` requires Kyverno ≥ 1.16 |

**Operations `CREATE, UPDATE`** were added explicitly — the original `ClusterPolicy` with `background: true` implicitly evaluated on both admission and background scans, so both operations are included to cover admission enforcement.

description: mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ MutatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that mutates Pods and Services to add the label 'foo: bar' to metadata.labels. The label must be added if absent and left unchanged if already present. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_add_default_labels.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-foo-bar-label
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["services"]
  matchConditions:
    - name: foo-label-absent
      expression: "!has(object.metadata.labels) || !has(object.metadata.labels.foo)"
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: |
          Object{
            metadata: Object.metadata{
              labels: {"foo": "bar"}
            }
          }
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what the policy does and why each piece is structured this way:

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — two entries to match both `pods` and `services` in the core API group (`""`), on CREATE and UPDATE operations.

**`matchConditions`** — a CEL guard that prevents the mutation from running entirely when the `foo` label already exists:
- `!has(object.metadata.labels)` — true when the resource has no labels map at all
- `|| !has(object.metadata.labels.foo)` — true when labels exist but `foo` is absent

CEL's `||` short-circuits, so the second term is never evaluated when labels are nil. This is what gives the "left unchanged if already present" guarantee — the mutation is never invoked for resources that already have `foo`.

**`mutations[0].patchType: ApplyConfiguration`** — the merge-style patch type. The CEL expression returns `Object{ metadata: Object.metadata{ labels: {"foo": "bar"} } }`, which Kyverno merges into the existing resource. Because `matchConditions` already gates execution, this expression only runs when `foo` is absent, and the merge semantics of ApplyConfiguration preserve all other existing labels.

description: generates a NetworkPolicy named 'default-deny' in any newly-created Namespace. The generated NetworkPolicy must select all Pods (empty podSelector), include both 'Ingress' and 'Egress' policyTypes, and define no ingress or egress rules so all traffic is denied by default.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ GeneratingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that generates a NetworkPolicy named 'default-deny' in any newly-created Namespace. The generated NetworkPolicy must select all Pods (empty podSelector), include both 'Ingress' and 'Egress' policyTypes, and define no ingress or egress rules so all traffic is denied by default. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_create_networkpolicy.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-default-deny-networkpolicy
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Default-Deny Network Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Multi-Tenancy, Network Security
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: NetworkPolicy
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.16.0"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Generates a default-deny NetworkPolicy named 'default-deny' in every
      newly-created Namespace. The policy selects all Pods (empty podSelector)
      and specifies both Ingress and Egress policyTypes with no rules, which
      denies all inbound and outbound traffic by default unless explicitly
      permitted by another NetworkPolicy.
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
    generateExisting:
      enabled: false
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:
          - ""
        apiVersions:
          - v1
        operations:
          - CREATE
        resources:
          - namespaces
  variables:
    - name: targetNs
      expression: object.metadata.name
    - name: downstream
      expression: |
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("NetworkPolicy"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("networking.k8s.io/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": "default-deny"
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "podSelector": dyn({}),
              "policyTypes": dyn(["Ingress", "Egress"])
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key design decisions:

**API shape**
- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` — the stable GA version introduced in Kyverno 1.17 (the task asked for 1.16+, and `v1` is the correct forward-compatible choice; `v1beta1` is the 1.16 beta form, but `v1` is fully valid per the docs)
- `kind: GeneratingPolicy` — cluster-scoped, can generate into any namespace

**Trigger**
- `matchConstraints.resourceRules` watches `namespaces` for `CREATE` operations only — this fires exactly once when a namespace is newly created

**Generated resource** (via CEL `variables.downstream`)
- Kind: `NetworkPolicy`, name: `default-deny`
- `podSelector: {}` — selects all Pods
- `policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]` — both directions declared
- No `ingress:` or `egress:` keys — omitting them (not even an empty list) produces an implicit deny-all for both directions per the NetworkPolicy spec

**Generation call**
- `generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)` — places the NetworkPolicy in the namespace that was just created
- `dyn(...)` wrappers are required around nested maps and lists in CEL object literals (CEL is statically typed; `dyn` opts into dynamic typing for nested structures)

**Evaluation settings**
- `synchronize.enabled: true` — Kyverno will reconcile the NetworkPolicy if it's manually deleted or drifted
- `generateExisting.enabled: false` — set to `true` if you also want the policy retroactively applied to pre-existing namespaces

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers add any Linux capability via securityContext.capabilities.add beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT).
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers add any Linux capability via securityContext.capabilities.add beyond the Pod Security Standards baseline allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT). Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_capabilities.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-capabilities
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Linux Capabilities
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Deployment,CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs whose containers, initContainers,
      or ephemeralContainers add Linux capabilities beyond the PSS baseline
      allow-list (AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL,
      MKNOD, NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT).
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:   [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["pods"]
      - apiGroups:   ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups:   ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["cronjobs"]
  variables:
    # Extract the pod spec regardless of the wrapping resource type.
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        object.kind == "Pod"
          ? object.spec
          : object.kind == "Deployment"
            ? object.spec.template.spec
            : object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec

    # Combine all three container collections into one flat list.
    - name: allContainers
      expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.containers
          + variables.podSpec.?initContainers.orValue([])
          + variables.podSpec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([])

    # PSS baseline allow-list for capabilities.add.
    - name: allowedCapabilities
      expression: >-
        [
          "AUDIT_WRITE", "CHOWN", "DAC_OVERRIDE", "FOWNER", "FSETID",
          "KILL", "MKNOD", "NET_BIND_SERVICE", "SETFCAP", "SETGID",
          "SETPCAP", "SETUID", "SYS_CHROOT"
        ]

  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.allContainers.all(container,
          !has(container.securityContext) ||
          !has(container.securityContext.capabilities) ||
          !has(container.securityContext.capabilities.add) ||
          container.securityContext.capabilities.add.all(cap,
            cap in variables.allowedCapabilities
          )
        )
      message: >-
        One or more containers add Linux capabilities not permitted by the
        Pod Security Standards baseline. Allowed capabilities are:
        AUDIT_WRITE, CHOWN, DAC_OVERRIDE, FOWNER, FSETID, KILL, MKNOD,
        NET_BIND_SERVICE, SETFCAP, SETGID, SETPCAP, SETUID, SYS_CHROOT.
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what the policy does and why each part is shaped the way it is:

---

### Structure overview

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`**
The 1.16+ CEL-native type. It uses Kubernetes' built-in `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` machinery under the hood instead of Kyverno's older JMESPath engine.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`**
Hard deny on admission (replaces the old `validationFailureAction: Enforce`).

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`**
Three separate rules to cover all three resource kinds at their correct `apiGroups`:
- `""` / `pods`
- `apps` / `deployments`
- `batch` / `cronjobs`

Operations are `CREATE` and `UPDATE` — background scans aren't needed here.

---

### Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `podSpec` | Ternary chain on `object.kind` to navigate to the pod spec (`object.spec` for Pods, `object.spec.template.spec` for Deployments, `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec` for CronJobs) |
| `allContainers` | Concatenates `.containers` (always present) with `.initContainers` and `.ephemeralContainers` using `?field.orValue([])` safe-navigation so missing optional fields default to `[]` |
| `allowedCapabilities` | The PSS baseline list as a CEL list literal for clean `in` membership tests |

---

### Validation expression logic

```
for every container in allContainers:
  securityContext absent  → pass
  capabilities absent     → pass
  capabilities.add absent → pass
  otherwise: every cap in capabilities.add must be in allowedCapabilities
```

The three `!has(...)` guards ensure the `all()` short-circuits safely when fields are missing, matching the same semantics as the old JMESPath `AnyNotIn` deny condition.

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that set spec.hostNetwork, spec.hostPID, or spec.hostIPC to true. All three host namespace fields must be checked.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that set spec.hostNetwork, spec.hostPID, or spec.hostIPC to true. All three host namespace fields must be checked. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_host_namespaces.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-namespaces
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Host Namespaces
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Deployment,CronJob
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Sharing host namespaces (hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC) grants privileged
      access to node-level resources and can be used for container escape or
      privilege escalation. This policy denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob
      that explicitly sets spec.hostNetwork, spec.hostPID, or spec.hostIPC to
      true.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:   [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["pods"]
      - apiGroups:   ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups:   ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["cronjobs"]
  variables:
    # Normalise the three resource shapes to a single pod spec so the
    # validation expressions below can be written once and reused.
    #
    #   Pod:        object.spec
    #   Deployment: object.spec.template.spec
    #   CronJob:    object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
    - name: podSpec
      expression: |
        request.kind.kind == "Pod"
          ? object.spec
          : request.kind.kind == "Deployment"
            ? object.spec.template.spec
            : object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
  validations:
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostNetwork.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        spec.hostNetwork must be unset or false. Enabling hostNetwork shares
        the node's network namespace with the container.
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostPID.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        spec.hostPID must be unset or false. Enabling hostPID shares the
        node's process ID namespace with the container.
    - expression: "!variables.podSpec.?hostIPC.orValue(false)"
      message: >-
        spec.hostIPC must be unset or false. Enabling hostIPC shares the
        node's IPC namespace with the container.
raw_log:
The policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the key design decisions based on the docs:

---

**API version & kind**
`policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `ValidatingPolicy` — the native CEL-based policy type introduced in Kyverno 1.16+, replacing the old `kyverno.io/v1` `ClusterPolicy`.

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — three separate rules to cover all three resource shapes:
| Resource | apiGroup | spec path to pod |
|---|---|---|
| Pod | `""` (core) | `object.spec` |
| Deployment | `apps` | `object.spec.template.spec` |
| CronJob | `batch` | `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec` |

**`variables.podSpec`** — a single CEL ternary expression normalises all three resource shapes into one variable, so the three validation rules are written once rather than nine times. It uses `request.kind.kind` (the admission request's GVK) to pick the right path.

**`validations`** — three separate expressions, one per field, using the optional-access + `orValue` pattern (`?hostNetwork.orValue(false)`) so that absent fields (the common case — they default to `false`) never cause a CEL evaluation error, only an explicit `true` triggers a deny.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`** — hard blocks on CREATE and UPDATE; no warnings-only pass-through.

description: denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies Pods, Deployments, and CronJobs that use any hostPath volume. The check must reject any volume in spec.volumes whose hostPath field is set, regardless of the hostPath value. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_host_path.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-host-path
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow hostPath
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod,Volume
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      HostPath volumes let Pods use host directories and files directly, which
      can be exploited to access sensitive host data or escalate privileges.
      This policy denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob that declares a volume
      with the hostPath field set, regardless of the path value.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["cronjobs"]
  # Extract the pod spec from whichever resource kind is being admitted.
  # Pods:       spec
  # Deployments: spec.template.spec
  # CronJobs:   spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
  variables:
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        object.kind == "Pod"
          ? object.spec
          : object.kind == "Deployment"
            ? object.spec.template.spec
            : object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.?volumes.orValue([]).all(vol, !has(vol.hostPath))
      message: >-
        HostPath volumes are forbidden. The field spec.volumes[*].hostPath must not be set.
raw_log:
The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of every design decision:

---

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`**  
The new Kyverno 1.16+ type — replaces the old `kyverno.io/v1` `ClusterPolicy`.

**`spec.validationActions: [Deny]`**  
Enforcing mode; the request is rejected on any match failure.

**`spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`**  
Three explicit entries cover all three resource kinds in their correct API groups:
- `""` / `pods`  
- `apps` / `deployments`  
- `batch` / `cronjobs`  

Both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations are checked so existing workloads can't be updated to add a hostPath volume.

**`spec.variables[0].podSpec`**  
A CEL variable that resolves to the pod-level spec regardless of which resource kind is being admitted. Because volumes live at different paths in each kind:
- Pod → `object.spec`
- Deployment → `object.spec.template.spec`
- CronJob → `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec`

…the ternary branches on `object.kind` to return the correct sub-object.

**`spec.validations[0].expression`**  
```cel
variables.podSpec.?volumes.orValue([]).all(vol, !has(vol.hostPath))
```
- `?volumes` — optional field access; returns an `optional<list>` so the expression is safe even when no volumes are declared.
- `.orValue([])` — falls back to an empty list, making the `all()` vacuously true (no volumes → passes).
- `.all(vol, !has(vol.hostPath))` — iterates every volume and asserts that the `hostPath` field is **absent**, satisfying the requirement to reject any volume where `hostPath` is set regardless of its value.

description: denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set securityContext.privileged to true.
prompt:
Write a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob whose containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set securityContext.privileged to true. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_disallow_privileged.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
- Community policy examples: https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno-policies (look at *-vpol/, *-mpol/, *-gpol/ directories for converted examples)
output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: disallow-privileged-containers
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Disallow Privileged Containers
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Pod Security Standards (Baseline)
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Privileged mode disables most security mechanisms and must not be
      allowed. This policy denies any Pod, Deployment, or CronJob whose
      containers, initContainers, or ephemeralContainers set
      securityContext.privileged to true.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
      - apiGroups: ["apps"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["deployments"]
      - apiGroups: ["batch"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["cronjobs"]
  variables:
    # Extract the pod spec from wherever it lives in each resource kind.
    # Pod:        spec
    # Deployment: spec.template.spec
    # CronJob:    spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
    - name: podSpec
      expression: >-
        object.kind == 'Pod'
          ? object.spec
          : object.kind == 'Deployment'
            ? object.spec.template.spec
            : object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
  validations:
    # --- containers ---
    - expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.containers.all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        )
      message: >-
        Privileged containers are not allowed. Set
        securityContext.privileged to false or omit the field.

    # --- initContainers (optional field; default to empty list) ---
    - expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.?initContainers.orValue([]).all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        )
      message: >-
        Privileged initContainers are not allowed. Set
        securityContext.privileged to false or omit the field.

    # --- ephemeralContainers (optional field; default to empty list) ---
    - expression: >-
        variables.podSpec.?ephemeralContainers.orValue([]).all(c,
          c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false
        )
      message: >-
        Privileged ephemeralContainers are not allowed. Set
        securityContext.privileged to false or omit the field.
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of every design decision:

---

## Policy design

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`**
The stable Kyverno 1.16+ policy type that supersedes `ClusterPolicy`. It is built on Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` and uses CEL throughout.

**`validationActions: [Deny]`**
Hard-deny on CREATE and UPDATE; no warn-only fallback.

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`**
Three explicit resource rules — one per group/resource, because the pod spec lives at a different path in each kind:
| Kind | API group | Pod spec path |
|---|---|---|
| Pod | `""` (core) | `spec` |
| Deployment | `apps` | `spec.template.spec` |
| CronJob | `batch` | `spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec` |

**`variables[podSpec]`**
A single CEL variable normalises the three different spec paths into one name (`variables.podSpec`) using nested ternary expressions, so the validation rules below it don't need to repeat the branching logic.

**Three `validations` entries**
Each targets one container list:
- `containers` — always present, so accessed directly with `.all()`.
- `initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers` — optional fields, so accessed with `?` (CEL optional-chaining) and `.orValue([])` to return an empty list rather than an error when the field is absent.

The predicate `c.?securityContext.?privileged.orValue(false) == false` safely handles missing `securityContext` or missing `privileged` — both are treated as `false` (not privileged).

output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-app-name-label
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require app.kubernetes.io/name Label
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Requires all Pods to have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a
      non-empty value. Admission is denied if the label is missing or empty.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["pods"]
  validations:
    - expression: >-
        object.metadata.?labels['app.kubernetes.io/name'].orValue('') != ''
      message: >-
        Pod must have the label 'app.kubernetes.io/name' set to a non-empty value.
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of the key design decisions:

**CEL expression:**
```
object.metadata.?labels['app.kubernetes.io/name'].orValue('') != ''
```
- `?labels` — optional field access; returns `optional<map>` so the expression is safe even when `metadata.labels` is entirely absent
- `['app.kubernetes.io/name']` — map key lookup on the optional result; propagates `optional.none()` if the key is missing
- `.orValue('')` — collapses any `optional.none()` to an empty string
- `!= ''` — the single condition that denies both the missing-label case and the empty-value case

**Enforcement:**
- `validationActions: [Deny]` — hard denial on admission (not just audit/warn)
- Covers both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations so the constraint can't be bypassed by patching an existing Pod

**Scope:**
- `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `resources: ["pods"]` — targets bare Pods only; add a second `resourceRules` entry or enable `spec.autogen` if you also want to enforce this on Deployment/DaemonSet/etc. pod templates

description: requires every container in a Pod to set spec.containers[].resources.requests.memory, spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu, and spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory. Deny admission if any container is missing any of these three fields. CPU limit is NOT required.

Write a Kyverno 1.16+ ValidatingPolicy (apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1) that requires every container in a Pod to set spec.containers[].resources.requests.memory, spec.containers[].resources.requests.cpu, and spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory. Deny admission if any container is missing any of these three fields. CPU limit is NOT required. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_require_resource_limits.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/

output_yaml:
apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-container-resources
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Require Container Resource Requests and Memory Limit
    policies.kyverno.io/category: Best Practices
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: medium
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: Pod
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: "1.16.0"
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Requires every container (including init and ephemeral containers) to
      declare resources.requests.memory, resources.requests.cpu, and
      resources.limits.memory. CPU limits are intentionally not required.
      Admission is denied if any container is missing any of these three fields.
spec:
  validationActions:
    - Deny
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups:   [""]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations:  ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources:   ["pods"]
  validations:
    - message: >-
        All containers must set resources.requests.memory,
        resources.requests.cpu, and resources.limits.memory.
      expression: >-
        object.spec.containers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          has(c.resources.limits.memory)
        )

    - message: >-
        All init containers must set resources.requests.memory,
        resources.requests.cpu, and resources.limits.memory.
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.initContainers) ||
        object.spec.initContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          has(c.resources.limits.memory)
        )

    - message: >-
        All ephemeral containers must set resources.requests.memory,
        resources.requests.cpu, and resources.limits.memory.
      expression: >-
        !has(object.spec.ephemeralContainers) ||
        object.spec.ephemeralContainers.all(c,
          has(c.resources) &&
          has(c.resources.requests) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.memory) &&
          has(c.resources.requests.cpu) &&
          has(c.resources.limits) &&
          has(c.resources.limits.memory)
        )
raw_log:
Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a breakdown of every design decision:

---

## Policy structure

**`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy`**
The new 1.16+ policy type, distinct from the legacy `kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy`. Uses CEL instead of pattern matching.

**`spec.validationActions: [Deny]`**
Blocks the request outright. `Audit` would only log — the task requires denial.

**`spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`**
Targets core API group (`""`), `v1`, Pods only, on `CREATE` and `UPDATE`.

---

## Three validation rules

Each rule maps to one container array. `spec.containers` is always present so it's checked unconditionally. `initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers` are optional fields, so each is guarded with `!has(object.spec.initContainers) ||` — the rule passes vacuously when the field is absent, only fires when it exists.

**CEL expression per container:**
```
has(c.resources) &&
has(c.resources.requests) &&
has(c.resources.requests.memory) &&
has(c.resources.requests.cpu) &&
has(c.resources.limits) &&
has(c.resources.limits.memory)
```
All six `has()` checks are needed because CEL will panic on field access if an intermediate field is absent. Notably, `has(c.resources.limits.cpu)` is **not** checked — CPU limits are explicitly excluded per the requirement.

prompt:

Deny any Pod whose containers use images from any other registry. Write the policy to /Users/shreyas/Nirmata/policy-bench/output/claude/gen_restrict_registries.yaml.

Before converting, look up the Kyverno 1.16+ documentation and examples:
- Migration guide: https://kyverno.io/docs/guides/migration-to-cel/
- Policy type docs: https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/
